         "slide_type":"intro",
         "title":"Introduction to Decision Trees",
         "paragraph":"Decision trees are a type of machine learning model used for classification and regression tasks. They work by learning a hierarchy of if/else questions that lead to a decision.",
         "key_points":[],
         "page_range":"",
         "visual_hint":"none",
         "image_id":null,
         "speaker_notes":""
      },
      {
         "slide_type":"concept",
         "title":"Asking Questions to Classify Animals",
         "paragraph":"Decision trees can be used to classify animals based on a series of questions. For example, asking whether an animal has fins can help distinguish between dolphins and bears. This process can be repeated with multiple questions to classify animals into different categories. As shown in Figure 2-22, a decision tree can be used to classify animals based on features such as having feathers, being able to fly, and having fins.",
         "key_points":[
            {"text":"Decision trees classify animals based on a series of questions","source_id":"Page 84"},
            {"text":"Questions can be repeated to classify animals into different categories","source_id":"Page 84"}
         ],
         "page_range":"Page 84",
         "visual_hint":"Figure 2-22",
         "image_id":"Figure 2-22",
         "speaker_notes":"Decision trees can be used to classify animals based on a series of questions"
      },
      {
         "slide_type":"concept",
         "title":"Continuous Data in Decision Trees",
         "paragraph":"Decision trees can also be used with continuous data. In this case, the tests used to split the data are of the form 'Is feature i larger than value a?'. This allows the decision tree to handle continuous features and make predictions based on them. For example, in a 2D dataset, the decision tree can learn to split the data based on the values of the features, as shown in Figure 2-23.",
         "key_points":[
            {"text":"Decision trees can handle continuous data","source_id":"Page 85"},
            {"text":"Tests used to split continuous data are of the form 'Is feature i larger than value a?'","source_id":"Page 85"}
         ],
         "page_range":"Page 85",
         "visual_hint":"Figure 2-23",
         "image_id":"Figure 2-23",
         "speaker_notes":"Decision trees can handle continuous data by using tests of the form 'Is feature i larger than value a?'"
      },

         "slide_type":"example",
         "title":"Random Forests for Smoother Boundaries",
         "paragraph":"Random forests can be used to combine multiple decision trees and produce smoother boundaries. By using multiple trees, the random forest can reduce overfitting and improve the accuracy of the model. As shown in Figure 2-33, the decision boundaries learned by a random forest can be much smoother than those learned by a single decision tree. This is because the random forest can average the predictions of multiple trees, leading to a more robust model.",
         "key_points":[
            {"text":"Random forests can reduce overfitting and improve accuracy","source_id":"Page 99"},
            {"text":"Random forests can produce smoother boundaries than single decision trees","source_id":"Page 99"}
         ],
         "page_range":"Page 99",
         "visual_hint":"Figure 2-33",
         "image_id":"Figure 2-33",
         "speaker_notes":"Random forests can be used to combine multiple decision trees and produce smoother boundaries"
      },
      {
         "slide_type":"example",
         "title":"Historical Development of RAM Prices",
         "paragraph":"Decision trees can be used to model the historical development of RAM prices. As shown in Figure 2-31, the price of RAM has decreased over time. A decision tree can be used to predict the price of RAM based on the date, by learning a hierarchy of if/else questions that lead to a prediction. For example, the decision tree can learn to split the data based on the year, and then make a prediction based on the average price of RAM for that year.",
         "key_points":[
            {"text":"Decision trees can be used to model the historical development of RAM prices","source_id":"Page 94"},
            {"text":"The price of RAM has decreased over time","source_id":"Page 94"}
         ],
         "page_range":"Page 94",
         "visual_hint":"Figure 2-31",
         "image_id":"Figure 2-31",
         "speaker_notes":"Decision trees can be used to model the historical development of RAM prices"
      },

         "slide_type":"concept",
         "title":"Controlling Complexity in Decision Trees",
         "paragraph":"Decision trees can be controlled by limiting the depth of the tree or the number of leaves. This can help prevent overfitting and improve the accuracy of the model. As shown in Figure 2-26, a decision tree with a limited depth can produce a simpler model that is less prone to overfitting. The decision tree can also be pruned to remove branches that do not contribute to the accuracy of the model.",
         "key_points":[
            {"text":"Decision trees can be controlled by limiting the depth or number of leaves","source_id":"Page 88"},
            {"text":"Limiting the depth or number of leaves can help prevent overfitting","source_id":"Page 88"}
         ],
         "page_range":"Page 88",
         "visual_hint":"Figure 2-26",
         "image_id":"Figure 2-26",
         "speaker_notes":"Decision trees can be controlled by limiting the depth or number of leaves"

         "slide_type":"example",
         "title":"Decision Tree Learned from Data",
         "paragraph":"A decision tree can be learned from data to classify examples into different categories. As shown in Figure 2-30, a decision tree can be learned from a 2D dataset to classify examples into two categories. The decision tree can learn to split the data based on the features, and then make a prediction based on the majority class in each region. For example, the decision tree can learn to split the data based on the value of the first feature, and then make a prediction based on the majority class in each region.",
         "key_points":[
            {"text":"A decision tree can be learned from data to classify examples","source_id":"Page 93"},

         ],

         "slide_type":"concept",
         "title":"Random Forests and Feature Selection",
         "paragraph":"Random forests can be used to select features by injecting randomness into the tree building process. By selecting a random subset of features at each split, the random forest can reduce overfitting and improve the accuracy of the model. As shown in Figure 2-34, the decision boundaries learned by a random forest can be much smoother than those learned by a single decision tree. This is because the random forest can average the predictions of multiple trees, leading to a more robust model.",
         "key_points":[

         "paragraph":"Decision trees are a powerful tool for classification and regression tasks. They work by learning a hierarchy of if/else questions that lead to a decision. Random forests can be used to combine multiple decision trees and produce smoother boundaries.",
